>
> Plant chloroplasts only get 3-6% efficiency from transfer of solar energy to the energy bonds of plant starches and fatty acids. Particular C-4 plants can get 7-8% (sugarcane) and one super cyanobacteria strain Chlorobaculum tepidum achieves 10%. Various modern fuel efficiencies are approximately -- for coal (~20-30s%) and solar (20%). This puts into perspective how important mitochondrial efficiency really is to optimal health. But it still begs the question...........what did Ling teach us about CM in mitochondria y'all missed?

People should read Gilbert Ling's 400-word summary of the Association-Induction hypothesis: [http://www.gilbertling.org/pdf/A 400 Word Summary of the Association-Induction Hypothesis.pdf](http://www.gilbertling.org/pdf/A%20400%20Word%20Summary%20of%20the%20Association-Induction%20Hypothesis.pdf)

Then, Ling's paper on Nano-plasma gets into some amazing detail: [http://www.gilbertling.org/PCP40\_ling\_ochsenfeld.pdf](http://www.gilbertling.org/PCP40_ling_ochsenfeld.pdf)

Basic summary:

The basic unit of life is what Ling terms the "nano-protoplasm". Cells do not need membranes to function.

Units of nano-protoplasm may vary, but all types have 6 things in common:

1.  a highly polarizable polypeptide chain
2.  abundant fixed anionic beta and gamma carboxyl groups on the polypeptide chain
3.  abundant CONH (carbonyl) groups on the polypeptide chain
4.  fixed cationic groups, which roughly match the number of anionic groups
5.  alternative adsorption partners for these groups -- K+ for carboxyl groups, Water for carbonyl groups
6.  ATP as principle cardinal adsorbent, specific sites that adsorb ATP, and auxillary cardinal sites that adsorb other controlling agents

Figure 2 in the paper shows how ATP adsorbs to the polypeptide chain, and unfolds the chain to expose water binding sites (carbonyl groups). The water that is bound here forms ordered layers -- ie: what Gerald Pollack has proven to be charge-separated water next to a hydrophilic substance.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

More generically, all you need is an Electron Withdrawing Cardinal Adsorbent (EWC) to unfold the polypeptide chain. ATP is the most powerful EWC, but the paper goes on to demonstrate experimentally that H+

Furthermore, Ling talks about the c-value of the beta and gamma carbonyl groups. The c-value is a measure of charge density, expressed in Angstroms. Angstroms are just a unit of length. A **low c-value** means that the length between these groups is small, and charge density is high (stronger bonding).

Ling notes that a **low c-value** K+ is preferred over Na+ (to bind with the carbonyl group). At high c-value Na+ is preferred over K+.

You get low c-values by having a strong EWC adsorbed to the polypeptide chain.

This is why when you see extracellular K+ (which is always paired with high intracellular Na+), you know something is very wrong. You are lacking a strong EWC to bind to polypeptide chains.

Another important note here is that Ca2+ is also a strong EWC needed to maintain the unfolded polypeptide chain structure. Whether or not we understand the exact mechanics of calcium efflux, the very fact that it happens indicates huge inefficiencies in the system. The fact that there is very little calcium adsorbed to the nano-protoplasm makes it even more problematic; calcium is a "large bang for your buck" ion, if you lose it, you lose a lot of your power.

Finally, I should note that these nano-protoplasm units can act coherently, forming a chain of units that collectively produce energy.
​

I don't know how this explains the basically 100% efficiency of mitochondria. What I write next in the next section below probably alludes to the 61% IR component, but not the other 39% ATP component.

When I wrote "units of nano-plasma vary" in the summary above, this usually refers to the type of polypeptide and the side-chain adsorbent. The example given by Ling was the red blood cell, for which the polypeptide is hemoglobin. We now know the magnetic difference between oxygenated hemoglobin (diamagnetic) and de-oxygenated hemoglobin (paramagnetic), and how those properties aid in blood transfer to tissues which need the O2. It's pretty obvious to me that this opens up a whole host of possible variation for specific functions mediated by the electromagnetic force.

I can still think of good reasons for a "cell membrane" -- pH control for example, or as a site for easier plasma double layer formation. But it isn't necessary per se.

\----

**On Rudolf the Red-nosed Reindeer**

Thornhill has something to say again. 8m50s to about 13m50s in the video below:

In that segment, he talks about the 'Red Square' nebula MWC 922, and how it is likely the result of a z-pinch discharge powered by plasma double layers.

A z-pinch discharge forms a cylinder of energy. I thought this pretty much fit the description of a cylindrical beam of red light coming out of Rudolf's nose.

The colour of the light that comes out likely depends on the energy in which electrons are accelerated in the plasma double layers. There are huge gamma ray bursts out there in the universe that look to be z-pinch discharges from very highly powered electrons. A less energetic discharge emits red light (again, Red Giant alert).

Here we are dealing with a maximum energetic threshold, and I can foresee the plasma double layer of mitochondria being only capable of producing red light because it is not energetic enough to produce anything blue-er.

And of course, that design of "only using the minimum amount of energy" is the exact definition of high efficiency.

Peter Mitchell was initially ridiculed for his theories on bio-energentics of mitochondria. Even though I pound the guy for screwing Ling.........he did one thing well. He was the first to ever show how effective endosymbiosis was in evolutionary history. He showed why protons matter. He showed that this change caused a pH gradient as well as an electrical charge of about 150 millivolts across the inner membrane. This may sound like a small amount of charge to most, but consider this fact I gave you long ago in a blog.
>
> The inner membrane is only 4-5 nanometers thick, so the voltage across this membrane is about 30 million volts per meter! For comparison, that is equivalent to the energy in a bolt of lightening. Electric arc chemistry also links UV photons and Xrays. I told you already we use those energies too..........but you have slept on those crumbs. That one bolt has the power generate energy of 1500 three thousand square foot homes in one mitochondria. Each cell has hundreds to thousands of those power houses in them. That is the power surge that fueled evolution and complex life forms and set the stage for UV repair and the eventual replacement by the DC electric current.

### nonchalant — 2014-11-06T02:19:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bio-hacking-eye-color.12033/post-146281>

If we are in poor health, we have to resort to using ATP for a primary source of energy. If we are in better health, we use ATP to uncondense proteins so we have more water bonding sites. Then we can use ambient IR to grow the water EZs to charge our water batteries.

If we have optimal redox, however, we uncouple the ETC in the mitochondria because we don't need ATP as much. We already have good EZs, so we use the heat generated by uncoupling the ETC to charge our water batteries all by ourselves.

Rudolph must be chowing down on lots of seafood (maybe sea veggies?) becaise his optimal DHA levels allow him to take sunlight and transform it to electrons for his ETC. But since he has optimal redox, he doesn't need so much ATP. He uncouples the ETC, and radiates IR. I'm guessing his high DHA levels on the inner mitochondrial membrane then transforms the excess IR back to visible light. Which is good for Santa, and good for his new reindeer buddies.

I really need to reread Tensegrity 7.

> You gain energy by eating food, by breathing oxygen, by exercise; by living you create energy. Man is a dynamo. He continuously creates energy. And when this energy accumulates in your being, you are uneasy, you want to throw it out -- because it feels like a burden. Sex is simply used as a relief. Now this is foolish.
> **_On the one hand, you go on working hard -- how to have better food, how to have more nourishment, how to have a better house, more rest -- on one hand, you want to have better air, more sun, more of the beach, more of the sky, more greenery: you work hard. Then you accumulate energy, you generate energy -- and then you are worried how to throw it somewhere, how to throw it down the drain. And when you have thrown it, again you are accumulating. This is a vicious circle._**
> From one end you go on accumulating energy, from another end you go on throwing it. This is the whole life! -- gathering energy, throwing energy; gathering energy, throwing energy. If this is all, then what is the point of it all? Why should one live? It is a repetition. It is a vicious circle. When energy is lost you are hungry for the energy; when energy is there you are ready to lose it. You find ways and means how to lose it.
> Buddha says that this is the most powerful thing in man's life. And if life is lived according to this, then life is a wastage, is a sheer waste. Nothing comes out of it. So much running, and never arriving anywhere. So much work, and no fulfillment. In the end comes death, and one finds one's hands are empty. Can this be the sole purpose of life? If this is the sole purpose of life, then life has no meaning, then life in itself is just accidental.
> One of the most profound thinkers of the West was G.K. Chesterton. He used to say that either man is a fallen god, or some animal has gone completely off his head. Only two are the possibilities: either man is a fallen god, or some animal has gone completely off his head. If sex is the only story, then some animal has gone completely off his head. There must be something more to it. There must be something more to life, otherwise it is meaningless. Your parents lived to give birth to you. You will live to give birth to a few more children, and they will live to give birth to somebody else. And this goes on and on -- but what is the purpose of it all?
> BUDDHA SAYS: By becoming aware, you open another door to energy. Sexual energy moves downwards; sexual energy moves towards the earth; sexual energy moves according to gravitation. When you become aware, there comes a change, a change of direction. The more aware you become, the more the sexual energy starts moving upwards -- it starts going against gravitation. It starts moving towards the sky. It starts moving on the lines of grace, not on the lines of gravitation.
> If sexual energy moves downwards, it is a wastage. If sexual energy starts moving upwards, you start exploding new worlds, new plenitudes of being, new altitudes of consciousness.

### Josh — 2014-11-16T19:54:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bio-hacking-eye-color.12033/post-147546>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=145743)
>
> So, getting good photochemistry means taking the best molecules (Chl a and b) that evolved in plants and making compliments in animals to this plan. I haven't seen any articles about better Chls being innovated in nature. I have seen article describing better organization of the stacking systems of Chl's, one using red and blue and the other using green....but, again, **_too much light absorption is a more common problem_** in plants, bacteria, and archea than too little, in most environments. What is the most common protein on earth? Rubisco is. What is that?
> **Rubisco = Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase. It** is an [enzyme](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enzyme) involved in the first major step of [carbon fixation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_fixation), a process by which atmospheric [carbon dioxide](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_dioxide) is converted by plants to [energy-rich](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fuel) [molecules](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Molecule) such as [glucose](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glucose). In chemical terms, it catalyzes the [carboxylation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carboxylation) of [ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate) (also known as _RuBP_). It is the most abundant [protein](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protein) on Earth and it is the mack daddy of the carbon system. RuBisCO is the most abundant protein in [leaves](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leaf), accounting for 50% of soluble leaf protein in [C3 plants](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C3_carbon_fixation) (20–30% of total leaf nitrogen) and 30% of soluble leaf protein in [C4 plants](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C4_carbon_fixation) (5–9% of total leaf nitrogen).
>
> Using Rubisco more efficiently in C4 plants vs. C3's is a partial solution for plants who grow in high light warm regions to protect water supplies in these plants. Might we use this idea to fix our gut microbiome?

> **Was photosynthetic RuBisCO recruited by acquisitive evolution from RuBisCO-like proteins involved in sulfur metabolism?**
> [Ashida H](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Ashida%20H%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=15950120)1, [Danchin A](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Danchin%20A%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=15950120), [Yokota A](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Yokota%20A%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=15950120).
> **[Author information](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15950120#)**
>
> **Abstract**
> Genome analyses have revealed that the genomes of non-photosynthetic bacteria including Bacillus subtilis code for proteins similar to the large subunit of RuBisCO (called RuBisCO-like protein (RLP)). This raises a fundamental question as to their functional relationship to photosynthetic RuBisCO. Recently, we identified the RLP of B. subtilis as the 2,3-diketo-5-methylthiopentyl-1-phosphate enolase in the methionine salvage pathway. In this mini-review, we suggest functional and evolutionary links between B. subtilis RLP and photosynthetic RuBisCO. Furthermore, we propose that photosynthetic RuBisCOs evolved from RLPs similar to that found in B. subtilis.

Could the RLB's in gut prokaryotes use Sulfur if necessary?

### Andrea — 2014-12-01T19:39:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bio-hacking-eye-color.12033/post-148946>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=145719)
>
> Why do people far away from the equator have blue eyes? They are reflecting blue light from their retina to protect DHA levels.
>
> Why do people who live on the equator have dark eyes? Deep brown or black is the color that best reflects all parts of the strong sunlight. When it is always strong you do not need a ton of light to signal.

That sounds plausible, but isn't it the other way around? I've always been taught that dark or black is the "color" that absorbs all parts of sunlight, while white is the "color" that reflects them all.

Still, I suppose there could be an advantage in having dark eye color near the equator. Having dark skin is. Place a white man and a black man in the sunshine - the white one reflects most of the light while the black one absorbs it. Who feels the best after a couple of hours?

Using a white (or blue or green) curtain for your bedroom will reflect light back out, but nothing beats a black curtain in making it dark in the room. Aluminium foil, maybe.  Hard to use that on eyes though...

### rlee314 — 2014-10-31T07:21:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-do-i-have-to-pee-a-ton-when-i-ct.12034/post-145747>

I'm sure this has been discussed before, so forgive me for asking again...I was getting some CT today-in the ocean. cool day here, cooler waters since seasons are changing. i was shivering multiple times. I wasn't drinking much water while i was at the beach, but every time i was in the ocean i would have to urinate multiple times.
I remember Dr. Kruse saying something like the body is getting rid of 'old' electron spent water? or something like that? maybe?

### ssj3 — 2014-10-31T09:46:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-do-i-have-to-pee-a-ton-when-i-ct.12034/post-145756>

From TENSEGRITY #5

> The more dense water is the more electrons it can carry. CSF is more dense by design. This happens by the action of your choroid plexus in your brain. This is why in cold temperatures make you urinate quickly. You are ridding your body of less dense water and structuring more dense water in your CSF naturally. This cycle occurs via water carried in your blood plasma. This is how you structure your own water naturally

### Da-mo — 2015-02-16T20:59:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-do-i-have-to-pee-a-ton-when-i-ct.12034/post-155809>

### yewwei.tan — 2015-02-16T23:46:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-do-i-have-to-pee-a-ton-when-i-ct.12034/post-155814>

Firstly, let's get the actual observations clarified.

For myself, just being in a cold environment increases the need to pee, but does not increase thirst at all while the cold stimulus is active, and thirst does not come back after the cold stimulus is lost.

"Cold environment" here includes just simply cold air. Example: I was walking around Tokyo in admitted was shorts and then a T-shirt plus jacket. It was around 2-13C most days, and I was basically stopping every hour to get to the toilet. I'd drinking 1.5L of water in the morning, and then end up pissing out 2.5L by lunch time (approx 5 trips to the toilet)

I personally do not see an increase in perceived thirst moving from a cold environment to a warm environment either. Never really felt like drinking too much after getting out of a cold tub or cold ocean. I'd feel thirsty something like 2 hours after, but how much that is due to the loss of cold stimulus I do not know.

\----

**What is the Ideal Mechanism?
**
Again, my mind wants to play god for a little while and see how I would construct biology if I wanted to create an energy efficient world.

From an energetics perspective, having as little water as possible, which is ideally 100% charge-separated, stuck in micro-tubules while performing quantum mechanical effects, is going to lead to the most energy efficient state.

If you ask me, the line of causality goes:

(1) Cold stimulus presents itself. Energy goes up systemically.

(2) "More energy" with respect to cold stimulus refers to greater magnetic effects, but the end goal is basically more hydrophilic proteins

(3) More hydrophilic proteins imply smaller distances between all components. Smaller distances imply less space for water relative to before the cold stimulus was presented.

(4) Water that cannot charge-separate literally gets "squeezed out" of the collagen matrix, ends up in plasma, and gets filtered out of the body by the kidneys.

(5) Charge-separated water tends to stay charge separated until a "stressor" comes in to destroy the EZ water sheets. The body probably doesn't need extra water for awhile after an energy-additive action, while is probably why I don't see thirst rise after awhile.
​

So long as the collagen matrix remains tight, the body literally needs less water to function compared to a looser state (which is probably the baseline state, and you need to take specific actions like CT to constrict the matrix some more). Drinking extra water in this scenario is not useful.

​

\-----
**Refeed Mechanism**

With insulin-spiking protein or carb refeeds, I find a simultaneous increase in thirst and in the need to pee.

Though this new lens, I view thirst as the drive to acquire more water as a direct result of the SIMULTANEOUS:

**(a)** increase in cellular information signalling between components

**(b)** increase in structured water in particular components, which allow for sensing of energetic gradients (Onsager's Relation). These gradients get relayed to the water balance sensors of the brain (PVN and then pituitary)​

With actions like CBD oil, nicotine gum, and bitter melon extract, I suspect the net effect to be an increase in respiratory uncoupling, which then leads to IR light generation to increase the EZ of water around the mitochondria and adjacent macroscopic components. This addresses point (b) above.

I then think that (b) can feedback onto (a), so basically you have increases in structured water increasing the ability to push light and electric signals through that now charge-separated water.

I think the additional benefit of transient protein/carb refeeding also has is that insulin potentially makes cell membranes more fluid and receptive to oscillations. This bolsters point (a) above.

I then view the need to pee as how much non-structured water you need to get rid off from the blood plasma.

We can then create the concept of "EZ water turnover rate". A successful refeed for me as always been measured by more water loss than increase in thirst. ie: I can satisfy my thirst, and still end up losing more water over time (this is a loss of mass).

.....

# Thread: Recipe for Sea Water
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipe-for-sea-water.12039/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2014-10-31T19:08:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recipe-for-sea-water.12039/post-145804>

What do you think? Comments?

For those of you that have a dedicated outdoor CT tub - would using this help keep the water clean longer? _Hint, hint Nonchalant. Want to be the tester?_

(Planning, planning, planning...)

**The Recipes for Sea Water**

**If you want to fill your bathtub** (40 gallons or 150 liters of water, your bathtub may vary), add
13 cups (3.12 liters) of salt,
4 cups (0.96 liters) of epsom salt,
1/3 cup (80 milliliters) of salt substitute,
and 5 teaspoons (25 milliliters) of baking soda.

**For a 5-gallon** (19 liters) batch of homemade sea water, add
1 and 2/3 cups (0.4 liters) of salt,
1/2 cup (120 milliliters) of epsom salt,
2 and 1/4 teaspoons (11 milliliters) of salt substitute,
and 2/3 teaspoons (3.3 milliliters) of baking soda.

**If you’d like to make a pint** (0.48 liters), add
2 teaspoons (9 milliliters) of salt,
2/3 teaspoons (3.3 milliliters) of epsom salt,
1/20 teaspoons (0.25 milliliters) of salt substitute,
and 1/62 teaspoons (0.08 milliliters) of baking soda.

Since the measurements in the pint version are ridiculously small, it would probably be easier to make larger batches of the stuff, and use a pint at a time. In general, and regardless of the measuring system or total amount required, you need
6,000 parts tap water,
125 parts salt,
38 parts epsom salt,
3 parts salt substitute,
1 part baking soda.

If you’d like, you can mix up large amounts of the dry ingredients, in the above proportions, and then scoop out 167 parts of the mixture to every six thousand parts water. For every gallon of water, you’d add a little more than 7 tablespoons of the dry mix (for every liter, add 28 milliliters of the mix). For the pint-sized mixture, add a shade more than 2 and a half teaspoons of dry ingredients.

Once a reliable resource on the ingredients of the Dead Sea can be found, “recipes” for that will be published here, as well.

[http://www.psorsite.com/docs/homeseawater.html](http://www.psorsite.com/docs/homeseawater.html)

# Thread: Lithium, could help reset circadian mismatch!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lithium-could-help-reset-circadian-mismatch.12043/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AJT — 2014-11-01T18:03:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lithium-could-help-reset-circadian-mismatch.12043/post-145864>

Several alternative docs like Dr Jonathan Wright and others have advocated lithium orotate for brain health. He recommends small doses of the lithium orotate.

I tried it and it does have what I feel is a measurable effect on a tiny dose of only 4.8mg. I tried to go through the data to find out why, and I was surpised that the mechanism is not well understood, then I came across these and I thought this forum would be a great place to discuss.

There has also been discussing on the neuro protective properties, as well as helping those with TBI.

**Lithium Impacts on the Amplitude and Period of the Molecular Circadian Clockwork**

-   Jian Li,
-   Wei-Qun Lu,
-   Stephen Beesley,
-   Andrew S. I. Loudon mail,

-   Qing-Jun Meng mail

-   Published: March 12, 2012
-   DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0033292

**Abstract**
Lithium salt has been widely used in treatment of Bipolar Disorder, a mental disturbance associated with circadian rhythm disruptions. Lithium mildly but consistently lengthens circadian period of behavioural rhythms in multiple organisms. To systematically address the impacts of lithium on circadian pacemaking and the underlying mechanisms, we measured locomotor activity in mice _in vivo_ following chronic lithium treatment, and also tracked clock protein dynamics (PER2::Luciferase) _in vitro_ in lithium-treated tissue slices/cells. Lithium lengthens period of both the locomotor activity rhythms, as well as the molecular oscillations in the suprachiasmatic nucleus, lung tissues and fibroblast cells. In addition, we also identified significantly elevated PER2::LUC expression and oscillation amplitude in both central and peripheral pacemakers. Elevation of PER2::LUC by lithium was not associated with changes in protein stabilities of PER2, but instead with increased transcription of _Per2_ gene. Although lithium and GSK3 inhibition showed opposing effects on clock period, they acted in a similar fashion to up-regulate PER2 expression and oscillation amplitude. Collectively, our data have identified a novel amplitude-enhancing effect of lithium on the PER2 protein rhythms in the central and peripheral circadian clockwork, which may involve a GSK3-mediated signalling pathway. These findings may advance our understanding of the therapeutic actions of lithium in Bipolar Disorder or other psychiatric diseases that involve circadian rhythm disruptions.

Lithium changes the way light resets the brain’s internal clock in a mouse
model of bipolar depression

Valecia Liew1, Joseph LeSauter2, Rae Silver1,2
1Department of Psychology, Barnard College, New York, NY
2 Department of Psychology, Columbia University, New York, NY

Lithium changes the way light resets the brain’s internal clock in a mouse
model of bipolar depression
Valecia Liew1, Joseph LeSauter2, Rae Silver1,2
1Department of Psychology, Barnard College, New York, NY
2 Department of Psychology, Columbia University, New York, NY
Key Words: Suprachiasmatic nucleus, circadian rhythm, lithium, bipolar disorder, light
\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
Acknowledgements: A very big thank you to my mentor Dr. Rae Silver for her endless patience
and guidance during each step of my project and to Dr. Joe LeSauter for all of his kind assistance
and input. My thanks also goes to the rest of the wonderful Silver Lab, to Professor Peter
Balsam, and my senior thesis class.
2
Abstract
Background: Lithium is the drug of choice for treating patients suffering from bipolar disorder
(BP), a disorder characterized by mood swings between mania and depression. While in the
manic state, individuals may be hyperactive, make poor decisions and experience difficulty
sleeping. Symptoms of a depressive episode include a loss of interest in formerly engaging
activities, persistent feelings of sadness, and fatigue. Though the exact pathway by which lithium
acts to counteract the symptoms of BP is unknown, we do know that lithium helps animals
entrain to a longer circadian rhythm. Studies have shown that patients with BP tend to have a
shorter circadian rhythm than the near 24h period shown by a normal individual and experience
difficulty synchronizing to the external light and dark (LD) cues of a 24h day. We hypothesize
that because light is such a potent entraining cue, lithium may interact with LD signals to enable
BP individuals entrain to the 24 hour day. One can test this hypothesis by examining locomotor
activity and the levels of FOS expression following a light pulse during the animal’s internal
night (as determined by onset of activity) or during the external night (as determined by the daily
light-dark cycle).
Method: In order to determine the effects of lithium on brain and locomotor behavior, mice were
placed in a 28 hour day consisting of 14:14 light:dark cycle. Animals were randomly divided into
groups and fed either control chow or lithium supplemented chow. After receiving a one light
pulse (LP) at 2 hrs after lights off (during the external night) or 2 hours after the start of their
endogenous nightly activity bout (internal night), animals were sacrificed. Wheel running was
measured and c-FOS expression in the SCN quantified. Under normal conditions, a nocturnal
animal will exhibit increased levels of FOS expression if exposed to a light pulse of even five
minutes during the nighttime.
Results: Measurements of wheel running activity indicated that ten of the fourteen mice housed
in LD 14:14 had two peaks of activity at periods ~24h and ~28h both before and during lithium
treatment. We assume that the 24h peak derives from the internal biological clock while the 28h
peak is in response to the external cues of the 14:14 LD cycle. During the period of lithium
administration, and the mean period of the highest activity peak increased significantly and two
of fourteen animals had the period of their highest activity peak switched from ~24h to 28h. FOS
expression in the SCN parallels analysis of the behavioral results. Mice on lithium had
significantly more FOS in the entire SCN and specifically in the SCN core, regardless of whether
they were sacrificed during their internal or external night. Lithium treatment decreased the
amount of locomotor activity, a side effect with unknown import.
Conclusion: Analysis of wheel running behavior and levels of FOS expression in the SCN
showed that animals on a lithium diet exhibited a greater response to the light pulse given in the
night and were better able to entrain to the longer 28 hour day. We conclude that lithium acts
upon an animal’s endogenous clock, thereby facilitating entrainment to a lengthened external
cycle.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-02T18:10:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lithium-could-help-reset-circadian-mismatch.12043/post-145927>

Lithium normally is not used in our biology. It appears not to have a fundamental natural biological role, which is not to say that lithium compounds do not have an effect. But you should stop and ask why is Lithium excluded from life naturally...........might it be too reactive and have a very low therapeutic index?

Lithium atomic size is a problem. It alters the cytoskeleton proteins I spoke about In TENSEGRITY 1 and can affect mitochondrial migrations. It is a major competitive inhibitor to most monovalent and divalent cations in our cells. This means it can directly induce apoptosis right off the bat. Lithium has specific effects in eukaryotic cells tied to cell growth and differentiation. This has huge implications according to Ling's work. Be careful...........when you don't understand the scale of biology Lithium is something to be careful with. The chemistry of lithium is unusual. Lithium atoms are very small, highly polarized, and have a high charge density. This charge density issue is why biology does not use Lithium naturally because it creates imbalances when you consider how a mitochondrion works using electrons. The chemical and biochemical properties of lithium are similar to those of magnesium, with which it shares a “diagonal relationship” in the periodic table. Li has an atomic weight of 3 and Mg is 12. Huge size difference leads to massive electrical changes. Most do not get this level of action.

Lithium is between H+ and Na+ in the periodic group 1 and is not part of the ionic plasma of metals I introduced you all to in Tensegrity 5-7. Why? Lithium use is akin to putting silverware in your microwave and trying to predict a good outcome. If the electric charge is low in mitochondria........you might get lucky.......or you might force apoptosis in bad mitochondria and deplete your stem cells.

Lithium (I), Magnesium (II), iron (III), and calcium (II) are also susceptible to competition with aluminum (III). Atomic size similarity is a dominant factor over the CHARGE identity concerning metal ion competition. Because of this charge issue, Lithium (I) and aluminum (III) substitution for magnesium (II), calcium (II), or iron (III) in physiological settings become possible and likely. Aluminum slows reactions times down and Lithium speeds them up in a big way. Again, the scale of action shows you how things work..............

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-02T18:12:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lithium-could-help-reset-circadian-mismatch.12043/post-145929>

Now I just talked about Al on FB.........so let us look at the other side of the electric equation: Aluminum is the third most abundant element of the earth's crust and, in the whole, its concentration in water is much larger than other biologically relevant cations like magnesium (II), iron (III) or zinc (II). In contrast with its availability, it is not an essential element for living organisms in earth. Moreover, it has been reported as a toxic agent. Being so abundant and toxic, one could ask why does not affect more dramatically living organisms? The main reason is its geochemical control, since aluminum reacts easily with silicon, the most abundant element in earth, forming aluminosilicates and bauxite with oxygen. In this way, aluminum bioavailability is reduced substantially. In the environment above it was likely more bioavailable However, humans have increased the bioavailability of aluminum introducing it into foodstuffs, pharmaceuticals, and water treatment. The acidification of water and soil, provoked mainly by acid rain, make aluminum more soluble and bioavailable. Aluminum (III), is a similar cation to iron (III) or magnesium (II), and as these two cations, it may easily get inside a living organism handed by different ligands like citrate and fluoride. It is also a very good Lewis acid catalyst. But the big difference, is that it exchanges ligands very slowly. This last feature is one of the most important reason for aluminum not being an essential bioelement for living organisms. For instance, one of the magnesium roles is to stabilize structures. Calcium role is to bind and cause signaling through out the cell. Magnesium binds weackly these structures, moving easily from one point to another as it is required by the reaction mechanism, acting as a catalyst. Aluminum (III), however, would bind these structures strongly, and then, due to the small rate at which it exchanges ligands, the reaction would slow down dramatically. Anytime reactions slow you age faster. Life has to be metastable. Aluminum makes it too stable.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-02T18:14:44+0200

Dialysis encephalopathy, where the patient treated by dialysis are exposed to high aluminum concentrations, are the most affected and Parkinson-dementia complex of Guam are some examples of maladies linked to aluminum (III) interference.

In 1994 Fasman's group proposed two mechanisms for aluminum (III) interactions with the Neurofibrilar peptide (NF), in which aluminum may bind to carboxylate residues, forming complexes, or to phosphorylated residues of the NF, where bonds are created. This creates stable chains within our protein polymers and does not allow for metastability. IT can causes protein misfolding because of this stability. This is why it is associated with many neurologic diseases that are associated with a lowered EEG voltage like AD.

### Jack Kruse — 2021-11-27T23:27:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lithium-could-help-reset-circadian-mismatch.12043/post-306735>

Lithium's atomic construction allows for a time clock slowed down that stimulates a protein called CRMP2. Lithium is used for bipolar disorder. CRMP2. has been found to be inactive in the bipolar patients' cells - CRMP2 is a protein that's associated with nerve cell communication. When lithium was added to hiPS cells generated from lithium-responsive patients, it was rectified, and CRMP2 activity was returned to normal. Lithium is capable of inducing a time change due to its unique atomic arrangement to induce relativity changes.

This suggests that the mechanism behind the bipolar disorder isn't necessarily always genetic, as we've previously assumed, but instead is an issue with how the CRMP2 protein is regulated in the cell over time scales. Lithium is very low in atomic mass. Mass links to gravity and gravity links to time. The stronger gravity becomes the more time dilates because time slows. More lithium makes the brain lighter. That lowers gravitational power in cells and time should speed up where lithium is deposited.
That's good news because it indicates that it could be possible to fix the problem with the right medication to fix the circadian mismatch by changing time to optimize CRMP2 function.

it is high time we realize that studying the lithium response could be used as a 'molecular can-opener' to unravel the molecular pathway of this complex timing disorder, that turns out not to be caused by a defect in a gene, but rather by the post-translational regulation (phosphorylation) of the product of a gene - in this case, CRMP2 caused by light to alter the clock mechanisms in neurons. Everything likes to live where it ages the most slowly and gravity will pull it there. With this disease, because it is caused by a broken clock altering gravity by using a lightweight atom can improve the timing of phosphorylation. Gravity & lithium both acts like a clock. All clocks measure the flow of entropy as space/time deforms. Gravity gives things with mass a tendency to go where time is slower. Lithium does the opposite in Bipolar disorder.

# Thread: Erich's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/erichs-journal.12044/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### erich53 — 2014-11-01T20:54:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/erichs-journal.12044/post-145880>

Hi All,

I am a 36 year old male living in Brooklyn who is constantly trying to improve and optimize his life.

I was morbitly obese most my life e.g. 350lbs when I was 19. For the last 5 years I have been around 200lbs and active thanks to what I learned from Mark Sisson and later Dave Asprey & the bulletproof movement.

I work until 4am three nights a week, but I can sleep in as long as I want to. What is the ideal sleeping schedule with a job like mine? Should I go to bed at 4am every day and sleep until I wake up by myself. Or should I always get up at lets ay 8am and go to bed by midnight except the days I work?

I feel like I sleep well, and the sleep tracking software I used told the same. I usually sleep 8 hours.

My bedroom is pitch black. Upon rising I turn on the red LED panel over my bed, take a cold shower, open my window, do a 100 breath meditation, take my supplements and do some mobility work followed by a bulletproof coffee. In the warmer month here in New York I try to get to my garden within 30 min of rising and lay in the sun on the ground. In the winter that is obviously not as easy/pleasant. For the colder month I use a Philips goLITE/blue light during the day.

I try to work out two times a week. More has me too sore and I lose motivation. It's usually Basketball or heavy lifting (I tried both 5x5 and body by science) I also foam roll at least twice a week and do some stretching.

I follow the bulletproof diet, but I am gradually implementing what I learned here as well. Now I usually have my first meal two or three hours after my bulletproof coffee. It's either eggs or seafood. In the winter a warm cup of bone broth. I do not drink alcohol, am gluten free and usually low carb
Once a week or two late at night I feel decision fatigue and I make bad food choices: french fries and/or ice cream

I drink at least three litres of water a day. usually from my berkey filter or german mineral water from a glass bottle.

I eliminated plastic containers from my household.

My computer and phone turn off when I go to sleep, I also have two greenwave EMF filter in my bedroom.

My libido is good, actually great if I manage to stay away from internet porn. it's just so convinient :/

I own a grounding bracelet and the EmWave 2,but havn't gotten into using them regularly.

If there is any more information you will deem useful, please let me know.

### Josh — 2014-11-01T21:35:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/erichs-journal.12044/post-145882>

Forgive me, I could not resist....welcome

### caroline — 2014-11-01T21:54:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/erichs-journal.12044/post-145884>

Hi and welcome Erich. My understanding is that a berkey filter doesn't do a very good job.....

You are doing lots of good things for yourself ....but your work schedule sucks.......

### Inger — 2014-11-02T09:32:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/erichs-journal.12044/post-145909>

get you a real women instead and get lots of oxy

# Thread: Body Temperature Regulation - Please Help
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-temperature-regulation-please-help.12045/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5

### rogerb02 — 2014-11-02T00:04:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-temperature-regulation-please-help.12045/post-145893>

Hi, my name is Roger, and I have an issue going on with my autonomic nervous system. I have had Lyme disease since 2010, and from what I understand the Lyme disease gives off a neurotoxin as it is killed which works to destabilize the balance of the Hypothalamus-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis. I have started to be much worse in the last month when I killed off some Lyme bacteria and it impacted my body very much.

My body temperature regulation is the most alarming of my autonomic nervous system issues. I have to keep the air conditioning on in the house and a couple fans on me. When it gets above 60 degrees, my mucous membranes dry out and my eyes burn very much. My feet have developed rashes and blisters (looks like eczema). My heart also beats extremely fast when I am in temperatures above 60 degrees.

When my body temperature is measured via a thermometer, it is around 97-98. I have been told by one practitioner it is as if my body is stuck in a perpetual hot flash. My other symptoms include head pressure on top of the head, dryness of mucous membranes, brain fog, exhaustion, tachycardia.

My body is out of balance, and I have recently started taking Maca Powder in order to help the hormones balance out naturally. Are there other supplements that work on the HPA axis?

Is this related to testosterone in the body?

Thank you for your thoughts!

### caroline — 2014-11-02T01:37:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-temperature-regulation-please-help.12045/post-145897>

health history? Family health?

where do you live? work? non native EMF? fake blue lite?

what do you eat? drink? water?

How is your sleep? do you get outside? grounding?

C/T???

Do you have Dr. K's book .... The Epi-Paleo RX .... it will give you a lot of context - the first two years of his blog

If you can manage it ..... become a gold member - you will have 24/7 access to all the webinars ...and Dr. K does a live Q&A every month when you can ask him anything

### rogerb02 — 2014-11-02T01:54:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/body-temperature-regulation-please-help.12045/post-145901>

Hi Caroline, thanks for the reply. I uploaded a picture.

I will start a journal in that section.

Health history - Lyme disease started in 2010. Neurological chronic Lyme. Treatment with antibiotics was not effective. Used herbs and a rife machine.

I live in Chester, VA. I live with my parents - I am basically house-ridden because of the temperature difficulties. Lyme disease also causes Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia syndrome, which I have.

I eat a Paleo diet - lots of cooked greens, carrots, broccolli. I only drink water (tap)

I will look into becoming a gold member. What is the cost?

I'm not sure what C/T means?

I'm having trouble understanding if my problem stems from hormonal issues or if the autonomic dysfunction is generated from Lyme.

age?

you play sports? execise/sports histoy?

# Thread: Is there an easier way to cool a bath than ice in a warm location - for CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-an-easier-way-to-cool-a-bath-than-ice-in-a-warm-location-for-ct.12046/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jeff Power — 2014-11-02T05:39:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-an-easier-way-to-cool-a-bath-than-ice-in-a-warm-location-for-ct.12046/post-145906>

I live in Scottsdale, AZ, hence my bath water does not come out cool. Instead of buying a million bags of ice to dump in the tub, does anyone have suggestions for easily cooling down the bath water?

Is there any type of device I could hook up to the bath faucet that would circulate the water through a cooling apparatus? Maybe using cold packs are another option? Or buying an ice machine, any suggestions?

Thank you for your responses in advance.

### caroline — 2014-11-02T09:43:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-an-easier-way-to-cool-a-bath-than-ice-in-a-warm-location-for-ct.12046/post-145912>

Randy has a great device ..... he has a pic somewhere .... maybe pm him?

### fitness@home — 2014-11-02T13:47:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-an-easier-way-to-cool-a-bath-than-ice-in-a-warm-location-for-ct.12046/post-145916>

There is a chiller with a pump that you can purchase and hook up. I think that is what Randy has. They are not cheap (I've looked) but if you are willing to make the investment. My husband works in an industrial setting and I always tell him to check with his vendors for me . Maybe I can get a used one that way.

Shah has a rubbermaid water trough that he sprayed with foam insulation on the outside. He lives in Florida and I think this really helps him keep the water cool. I think one of our old members, Jonathan, bought an ice machine.

I freeze empty spring water gallon containers for my CT ice. I have 4 of them in my inside freezer and another 6 in the garage freezer.

### Jeff Power — 2014-11-03T00:50:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-an-easier-way-to-cool-a-bath-than-ice-in-a-warm-location-for-ct.12046/post-145981>

Ok, thanks for the suggestions.

I'm going to look into a chiller with a pump or I'll probably just buy a separate freezer, I like the idea of using the gallon jugs. That is a great idea. I'll report back with my successes or failures...

### nicld — 2014-11-03T16:13:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-an-easier-way-to-cool-a-bath-than-ice-in-a-warm-location-for-ct.12046/post-146029>

I use the frozen jugs too and I have a smaller bucket that I freeze ice in and store in my deep freezer. I also purchased some reusable ice packs that were suppose to be leak proof for they lied. Lucky for me I live in WI and now that the night are getting colder I can set water outside to make ice.

### QuantumRadical — 2014-11-06T18:41:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-an-easier-way-to-cool-a-bath-than-ice-in-a-warm-location-for-ct.12046/post-146349>

Yes, get an aquarium chiller and pump. Get the biggest capacity one you can afford. Mine cools the water at about 6 degrees F per hour. This is the one I got.
[Hydroponic Chiller - Refrigeration Unit - 250-1200 Gal/Hr. - 1/2 HP - 4100 BTU - 360 Watt - 110-120 Volt - Use with 1/2 3/4 1 in. Tubing - Active Aqua](https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B000TMC2CI/ref=oh_aui_detailpage_o08_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1)

[Hydrofarm AAPW550 550-GPH Active Aqua Submersible Pump](https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B002JPIQ1Y/ref=oh_aui_detailpage_o07_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-06T18:48:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-an-easier-way-to-cool-a-bath-than-ice-in-a-warm-location-for-ct.12046/post-146350>

geothermal cooling. is very effective

### Jeff Power — 2014-11-07T15:35:48+0200

# Thread: Flight choice

### nicld — 2014-11-02T19:15:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-choice.12048/post-145939>

The DH and I are planning a trip to Costa Rica in April and we are still deciding on flight.

It just dawned on me yesterday that if we choose the shortest flight time there might not be enough time to get a proper supper at the airport during the layover. We would not arrive in Costa Rica until 8:30 pm (dark there already) and by the time we got done with everything it was be way late to eat.

There is a flight that leaves at a time that we can leave and has a longer layover time (enough for a decent meal) but is a longer travel time.

So, after making sure that I do the proper prep for flying would it be better to take the longer travel time to make sure that I get a chance to eat a proper meal at the proper time or take the shortest flight and just try to grab something during the short layover?

I already checked restaurants at both airports that the layover may occur and both have seafood establishments. Newark has a seafood place with fresh eastern seafood and Houston has an oyster bar in the airport.

### Inger — 2014-11-02T20:44:32+0200

Just eat seafood at the airport....  that way it will not be too late. I would not stress too much about eating a hour or two later just for one day  you need that DHA! if you can get salmon sashimi that would be awesome

### nicld — 2014-11-02T21:03:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-choice.12048/post-145951>

### nicld — 2014-11-03T15:57:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flight-choice.12048/post-146024>

Well, the hubby and I talked and we decided that it would be worth the extra flight time in order to make sure that we had a good meal during the trip down. Now to just wait to see which one of the two flights there gets lowest in cost.

# Thread: CT on the head, brain, face - Wow
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-on-the-head-brain-face-wow.12050/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jeff Power — 2014-11-03T00:57:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-on-the-head-brain-face-wow.12050/post-145982>

I put cold packs wrapped around my head today for an hour and it was actually invigorating!!

Anybody ever experiment with CT on the head or face? I know that there is a significant amount of mitochondria and these areas would probably benefit by regular CT.

Any recommendations or thoughts, or words of caution?

PS
On a side note I was listening to a podcast and Tony Robbins, who uses CT everyday, is investing in a cold therapy machine that allows his head to be inserted. Interesting stuff...

### Inger — 2014-11-03T08:30:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-on-the-head-brain-face-wow.12050/post-146005>

I like cold on head too...  I sometimes sleep with icepacks on my pillow and I could swear I look younger next morning
I dunk my face in cold water every morning too... i love it...

### Da-mo — 2014-11-03T11:17:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-on-the-head-brain-face-wow.12050/post-146015>

I think you need to be careful around the eyes

### Danco3636 — 2014-11-03T22:38:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-on-the-head-brain-face-wow.12050/post-146051>

I do ice face dunks almost daily.... love them. I also open my eyes up and look around in the ice water.... My eye sight at 50 is great! I also do better when I CT if I do a quick head dunk in the water too.....

### Jeff Power — 2014-11-04T03:23:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-on-the-head-brain-face-wow.12050/post-146062>

I might try the daily face dunks.

I was thinking about building a mask constructed out of a ski mask and lining it with cold packs so that it is a wearing head ice pack. If I accomplish this, I'll post some pics. I'll probably look funny but will feel great!!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-on-the-head-brain-face-wow.12050/post-146063>

I like face dunking before I submerge in a cold bath. I think it really gets the brain going. As I recover from some brutal skin treatment on my face in a couple of weeks, I am going to go heavy with the CT on the recovering areas. (See my post in Ask Jack).

### Jeff Power — 2014-11-07T15:37:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-on-the-head-brain-face-wow.12050/post-146435>

I just bought an ice pack made for the head. It is really for headaches. Ironically, all the people writing reviews have no idea that they are doing more good for themselves than just relieving their migraines, ha.

### seanb4 — 2015-04-28T14:54:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-on-the-head-brain-face-wow.12050/post-162643>

I have found when doing cold showers, if I stick my head directly under the shower head and go through the 1-2 minutes of something like brain freeze, the rest of the shower goes easier and I feel I warm up faster. Maybe this will cause the cold pathway to open up quicker since the brain will be directly experiencing the cold...

# Thread: Green Tea Chicken Soup
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-chicken-soup.12051/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2014-11-03T02:04:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-tea-chicken-soup.12051/post-145985>

Started with someone elses recipe and made it my own by tweaking it a bit.

Ingredients:
\- 3 green tea bags
\- 5C water
\- 1 sliced lemongrass stalk
\- 1 cup shelled edamame
\- 1 oz dried mushroom, stir fry style (My tweak. Recipe called for 1C sliced shitake)
\- 1 lb cooked shredded chicken
\- 1.5C chicken broth
\- 1 TBL duck fat (recipe called for sesame oil)
\- 2 sliced green onions
\- cilantro for garnish

Directions:
Heat 5 cups of water until boiling. Add teas bags, lemongrass and steep for 10 minutes. Remove tea bags and simmer. Add edamame and salt & pepper to taste.

I combined the dried mushrooms with 1.5C warm chicken broth and let them soak for 10 minutes; then added broth with mushrooms to the soup. Add chicken and green onion then simmer for a few minutes. The final touch was adding duck fat.

Picture doesn't do it justice...

# Thread: billy - Optimal Journal 11-2-14
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/billy-optimal-journal-11-2-14.12052/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 31
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### billy — 2014-11-03T02:35:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/billy-optimal-journal-11-2-14.12052/post-145987>

55 yo male
Seattle/Bellevue, WA
27 miles (30 mins.) to Puget Sound salt water beach.
1977 graduated- Honors, blew off a scholarship for music.
1978 read The Prophet, Siddhartha, Grist for the Mill = light bulb on.
1978 started eating whole food, then vegan, organic mostly.
1979 Quit music, got a straight job - construction electrician.
1980 pescatarian (vegetarian + fish).
1985 quit partying - zero alcohol/drugs since 1985.

1986 Dr said I had protein/blood in my urine - otherwise fit. No diagnosis, assumed it was from lifting heavy weight at work.

1988 moved from \*Seattle to the \*\*forest: Well water, surrounded by forest on acreage.
\* I had been getting very sick 3x a year missing days of work while in Seattle.
\*\*Didn't get sick for 5 years after moving to the country!!! (EMF only at work & inside new house).

1998 my son's protein needs caused me to add chicken breast, some beef - to diet.
1999 started racing motocross -> lift weights + cardio to survive it.
2002 Moved to Seattle-Bellevue suburb. Small house, pier & post foundation, re-wired, re-plumbed in Cu, fluorescent lights in most rooms, distribution level power line 100' away - not transmission level voltage.

2004 Wife bailed on the "wife & mother" thing and I was a single dad. Much stress, forget myself & focused on my son. Celibate for years, no dates, etc. Best thing that ever happened to me but I never would've imagined it would be! Quit racing MX and exercising.

2005 Blood pressure & blood sugar getting a bit high. Eating comfort food now most nights. Getting pudgy.
2008 Got married in Thailand to my friend's wife's best friend.
I had been going to Thailand regularly since 1992 - familiar w/ her culture. She was raised on a rice & duck farm in a village.
2010 Lipid panel, blood pressure & blood sugar looking borderline metabolic syndrome. Low libido.
2011 back to gym w/ 20 min. cardio before & after resistance training. Blood work improving.
2012 Libido still low & blood work staying the same.
2012 June started lifting heavy, bodybuilding routine. Dr checked testosterone level - LOW
2013 Started eating grass fed organic beef, taking nettle, ginseng, etc. to raise Test. level naturally
2014 Went on TRT, 1st with an Endo that didn't prescribe an aromatase inhibitor and had my injections 10 days apart. My E2 was high and test would crash.

Went to naturopath for TRT & levels are great now with .5mg Anastrozole and .4 ml weekly. Feel great!

Diet on KETO;
<50g carbs, 1.1 to 1.2g protein, remainder was healthy fat.
All organic food whenever possible-
Grass fed beef and chicken thigh. MCT, we cook in coconut oil, red leaf lettuce, steamed broccoli, spinach, carrots, spring onion, cilantro, parsley, tumeric, garlic, eggs, Atkins bars, kale, Swiss chard,Creamy yogurt. Sauerkraut, 1 gal. water daily

\*no wheat, dairy, or nightshades\*

Diet, off KETO 9-23-14;
Grass fed beef.
Chicken breast instead of thigh.
Lots more veggies - same ones as above.
Oatmeal + almonds, pumpkin seeds, raisins, cinnamon & Kerry Gold butter.
Brown Cow creamy yogurt. Eggs. Sauerkraut, > 1 gal. water daily. Oysters. Wild salmon.

Health history:
1-14-14 Son got MRSA after visit w/ birth mother
1-19-14 I got dizzy enough to note it on the calendar
3-30-14 I got PVD posterior vitreol detachment (saw flashes of light peripherally in left eye, had soot-like floaties)
4-12-14 start KETO after months of study and slowly tapering my macros toward KETO.
9-20-14 Dr had me stop KETO as did my ND. Bad lipid blood work;

LIPID PANEL:

Cholesterol, Total 315 **HIGH** (range is 100-199 mg/dL)

Triglycerides 56 (0-149 mg/dL) fasted - - So this is GOOD NEWS! Low risk of cardiovascular disease. On 8-26-14 Trig was 63 not fasted, on 9-20-14 Trig was 67 fasted. All under 150!

HDL Cholesterol 71 on 8-28-14. HDL 69 on 8-26-14 not fasted. HDL 62 on 9-25-14.(>39 mg/dL)
According to ATP-III Guidelines, HDL-C >59 mg/dL is considered a negative risk factor for CHD. - - So this is GOOD NEWS! Low risk of cardiovascular disease.

VLDL Cholesterol Calc. 11 (5-40 mg/dL) - This is GOOD NEWS! Low risk of cardiovascular disease.

LDL Cholesterol Calc. 233 HIGH (0-99 mg/dL) - **BAD** NEWS... calculated

C-Reactive [protein](http://www.synthetek.com/products/synthepure-whey-protein-isolate/) is .8 mg/L (range is 0-3.00) - GOOD NEWS! Low risk of cardiovascular disease.

My Apolipoprotein-B is 145 , (range is 0-70) - **REAL BAD**...? Way over range anyway. And on 9-26-14 Apo-B was 160 by calculation.

Non-HDL 9-26-14 was 259

LDL-C is 206

LDL-P is 2,458
LDL-R (real) -C = 191 on 9-25-14

Apo-B not higher than LDL = good

Insulin Resistance score = 25 (27 is low, 45 is mid, over 63 is high)

Trigs/HDL = poor man’s insulin resistance test; 62/71 = .873

IR on 8-26-14 = .913 not fasted

IR on 8-28-14 = .788 fasted

IR on 9-20-14 = 1.03 fasted

Average LP IR score is .91 insulin resistance ratio. A ration of 3+ is in trouble; mortality, type2 diabetes, CVD.

A1c = 5.5 then 5.4

On Niacin now w/ steam bath after to expel.
(should I go to Ezetamide? Niacin can worsen sterile absorption.)

GOAL:
Avoid statins!!!
Get back on KETO
(should I retry keto w/ less protein and no Atkins bars, more veggies?)

### billy — 2014-11-03T03:01:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/billy-optimal-journal-11-2-14.12052/post-145990>

PROTOCOL:
Start 11-1-14
Barefoot in cold wet grass several times a day.
Cold bath water dip.
Use incandescent lights, reduce fluorescent lights.
Turn brightness down on monitor, turn off TV more.
Get in the sun even more than I did - when it's out.
Wade in Puget Sound salt water 11-2-14 start

STRESS:
Work- moderate with high stress almost every day for a while, sometimes multiple times a day.
Home - very peaceful, zero stress or anger w/ wife. The usual w/ a teenage son, but no yelling & screaming.

### billy — 2014-11-05T03:54:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/billy-optimal-journal-11-2-14.12052/post-146161>

... great start on your journal!
\-Thanks caroline!

have you read Dr. K's book? The Epi-Paleo RX - gets you up to speed on the first two years of the blog
\-Just got it in the mail 11-3-14... that's why I had to stop reading here so much. It's edifying, as far as I've gotten - I've watched hours of Dr. K interviews on youtube & podcast.

have you listened to Jack's podcasts?
Many, but not enough -need more!

read his blogs?
\-Which, the Greek or the Chinese? lol - I have to look up every-other word
\-I tend to look up every study, source, Dr, author, researcher -that Dr. K mentions ...my brain hurts and I feel like I'm getting very little traction.

I am not sure where you are at exactly.....
\-Still researching but; Weekends wading in salt water, barefoot in the cold wet grass several times a day, nixed the fluorescent for incandescent lights, blue-blocker glasses came today, oysters daily, cold baths. No sun here now so tanning bed?

are you eating epi paleo with loads of seafood?
\-Getting there. I'll add more salmon again but oysters and grass fed organic beef daily. Been onto whole food for decades, was vegetarian then pescatarian for many years. I ate keto and studied it for months prior, so I'm hip to paleo tangentially.

non native EMF?
\-Reducing. I've thought EMF was bad on a cellular level for decades - avoided transmission lines and close proximity to transformers, etc. People think I'm a tin-foil hatter, as they head out to line up at McDonalds.

fake blue lite?
\-Turned my monitor brightness down, switched to incandescent from fluorescent lights - I used to think I was saving the planet, now I'm saving my butt. After that maybe I'll rule Earth. One thing at a time for me. lol

are you drinking R/O water?
\-Yes, over 1 gallon daily. I quit the filtered tap water which was < 1/2 my intake. Now I drink RO or natural spring water exclusively.

Thanks for your interest caroline. Off to read Epi Paleo RX in dim incandescent light now... hoping for traction.

I wonder why **Dr. Shui Yin Lo** hasn't been mention about Water Chemisty & Quantum Biology ?
5th Phase Of Water

He''s been researching water & quantum biology for more than 3o years. Dr. Shui Yin Lo received his Bachelor of Science in Physics from the University of Illinois in 1962, with highest honors and his PhD. in Physics from the University of Chicago in 1966, under the theory group lead by (2008) Physics Nobel recipient, Yoichiro Nambu. (Dr. Nambu is considered one of the leading figures in the development of modern particle physics.)
the “Baser”, of which Dr. Lo is the inventor and patent holder, produces a high-energy beam of enlarged particle clusters, thousands of times more powerful than the world’s most powerful laser. The U.S. Department of Energy, the Office of Technology Development, as well as the Los Alamos National Laboratory collaborated in this research. In 2005, the Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded to Roy Glauber at Harvard, for the theoretical calculations of optical coherence, the basis for Dr. Lo’s invention of the Baser.

**Water Chemistry Research:**[http://www.stablewatercluster.net/research/](http://www.stablewatercluster.net/research/)

[http://www.fkog.uu.se/course/a/a5/a5homeopati1.pdf](http://www.fkog.uu.se/course/a/a5/a5homeopati1.pdf)

identified, and characterized a unique type of stable (non-melting) ice crystals that maintain
an electrical field.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-11-10T04:21:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.12054/post-146706>

**Potassium** is a [chemical element](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical_element) with symbol **K** (derived from [Neo-Latin](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neo-Latin) _kalium_) and [atomic number](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atomic_number) 19. Elemental potassium is a soft silvery-white [alkali metal](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alkali_metal) that [oxidizes](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Redox) rapidly in [air](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth%27s_atmosphere)and is very reactive with [water](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water), generating sufficient heat to ignite the [hydrogen](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen) emitted in the reaction and burning with a [lilac](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lilac_\(color\)) [flame](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flame). Naturally occurring potassium is composed of three isotopes, one of which, 40K, is radioactive. Traces (0.012%) of this isotope are found in all potassium, making 40K the most common radioisotope in the human body and in many biological materials, as well as in common building substances such as concrete.

Because potassium and [sodium](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sodium) are [chemically](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical) very similar, their [salts](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Salt_\(chemistry\)) were not at first differentiated. The existence of multiple elements in their salts was suspected in 1702,[\[3\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Potassium&printable=yes#cite_note-1702Suspect-3) and this was proven in 1807 when potassium and sodium were individually isolated from different salts by [electrolysis](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrolysis). Potassium in nature occurs only in ionic salts. As such, it is f**ound dissolved in [seawater](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seawater)** (_which is 0.04% potassium by weight_), and is part of many [minerals](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mineral).

You know that life began in the oceans.........right?

Most industrial chemical applications of potassium employ the relatively high [solubility](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solubility) in water of potassium compounds, such as potassium [soaps](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soap). Do you think this is why K+ and water are linked in the sea by evolution and are in your cells?

A paleo leader recently gave a live video feed to a group in Europe. One question that raised was about sustainability and the paleo template. He answered very eloquently, describing soil erosion and how cattle grazing is better for the soil than not etc. You need to realize that was the earth's surface has more water surface than land. The paleo leaderships and ancestral health just do not get it. Land based herbivore's just don't have enough DHA. This evolutionary story is about how DHA works in changing sunlight into the DC electric current. Marine food chain has way more DHA in than pastured herbivore's. More surface water means more seafood for mankind and more DHA. You would think this evolutionary linkage would have hit their sensibilities, but it did not and still has not. It only makes common sense, evolutionary sense, and scientific sense to promote sea conservation and aquaculture as much if not more for our dietary needs.

The cow sustainability issue is 100% paleo bullshit in my view. And I want people to realized why it is.......water dominates the surface of our pale blue dot and science has shown that life originated within the seas, not a cow pasture 10,000-50K years ago. In 2012 at AHS, Remko Kuipers has been saying the same things as I have, but too tactfully really, in my view. People need to review that talk and see how many empty seats where at his talk at a paleo conference. He called out Cordain, and he has not worked with him since. Kuipers tweets after that AHS were classic.........he asked where were the talks on man's evolution from East Africa and the talks about seafood and not bacon?

Those things don't support the business model and religion.

On a global scale, the earth has more food, and can produce more food pound for pound in a healthy ocean. If paleo cared about the sustainability they would tell you more about DHA and would be looking at oceanic health more closely instead of selling Paleologix fish oil. We might actually begin to understand the coupling and linkage of the sea to eutherian mammal sustainability rise automatically as ocean health improved. Instead, ancestral health talks the nonsense of over grass fed cows while breeding them in poor conditions compared to how cows naturally live............ we wouldn't and should not need to worry about land based sustainability when we realize DHA is why grass fed meat works........and seafood is away better source for us all.

> **Potassium** is a [chemical element](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical_element) with symbol **K** (derived from [Neo-Latin](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neo-Latin) _kalium_) and [atomic number](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atomic_number) 19. Elemental potassium is a soft silvery-white [alkali metal](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alkali_metal) that [oxidizes](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Redox) rapidly in [air](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earth%27s_atmosphere)and is very reactive with [water](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water), generating sufficient heat to ignite the [hydrogen](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hydrogen) emitted in the reaction and burning with a [lilac](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lilac_\(color\)) [flame](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flame). Naturally occurring potassium is composed of three isotopes, one of which, 40K, is radioactive. Traces (0.012%) of this isotope are found in all potassium, making 40K the most common radioisotope in the human body and in many biological materials, as well as in common building substances such as concrete.
>
> Because potassium and [sodium](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sodium) are [chemically](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical) very similar, their [salts](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Salt_\(chemistry\)) were not at first differentiated. The existence of multiple elements in their salts was suspected in 1702,[\[3\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Potassium&printable=yes#cite_note-1702Suspect-3) and this was proven in 1807 when potassium and sodium were individually isolated from different salts by [electrolysis](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrolysis). Potassium in nature occurs only in ionic salts. As such, it is f**ound dissolved in [seawater](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Seawater)** (_which is 0.04% potassium by weight_), and is part of many [minerals](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mineral).
>
> You know that life began in the oceans.........right?
>
> Most industrial chemical applications of potassium employ the relatively high [solubility](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solubility) in water of potassium compounds, such as potassium [soaps](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soap). Do you think this is why K+ and water are linked in the sea by evolution and are in your cells?

Thanks Jack, this is very interesting that it is the most common radioisotope in the human body and is formed by salts.
form wikipedia
"In [chemistry](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemistry), **salts** are [ionic compounds](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ionic_compound) that result from the [neutralization](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neutralization_%28chemistry%29) reaction of an [acid](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Acid) and a [base](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Base_%28chemistry%29)."

I wonder if fusion is possible across the mitochondrial exclusion zone through dpe (dense plasma focus). The wider the EZ the higher energies capable causing different isotopes to form, similar to the interaction of the sun and the corona, or ionosphere and van allen belt.
Lilac flame= high energy electrons and high energy photons. This would tie summer electrons to synthesis of heavy metals for... sensing higher frequency emf.... who knows... you got my wheels spinning... I need to bring it back in. I have a lot of reading to do about solubility, soap and science.

### bionaut — 2014-11-12T04:14:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/meditation.12054/post-147078>

There seems to be a relationship between potassium and calcium. They are right next to each other in the periodic table. Potassium 40 seems to decay into calcium 40.

Here is some info from wikipedia.
**Potassium-40** (**40K**) is a [radioactive isotope](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radioactive_isotope) of [potassium](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Potassium) which has a very long [half-life](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Half-life) of 1.248×109 years. It makes up 0.012% (120 [ppm](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parts-per_notation)) of the total amount of potassium found in nature.

Potassium-40 is a rare example of an isotope that undergoes all three types of [beta decay](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beta_decay). About 89.28% of the time, it decays to [calcium-40](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calcium-40) (40Ca) with emission of a [beta particle](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beta_particle) (β−, an [electron](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electron)) with a maximum energy of 1.33 [MeV](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electron-volt) and an [antineutrino](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antineutrino). About 10.72% of the time it decays to [argon-40](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Argon-40) (40Ar) by [electron capture](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electron_capture), with the emission of a 1.460 MeV [gamma ray](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gamma_ray) and a [neutrino](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neutrino). Very rarely (0.001% of the time) it will decay to 40Ar by emitting a [positron](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Positron) (β+) and a neutrino.[\[1\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Potassium-40#cite_note-Engelkemeir-1)

[http://jackkruse.com/energy-and-epigenetics-3-autoimmunity-cancer-autism/](http://jackkruse.com/energy-and-epigenetics-3-autoimmunity-cancer-autism/)

Excerpt:
The point of paramount importance in this study, issued during the summer of 1979, is the fact that each one of over 60 experiments shows an increase in calcium in oats, germinated in an environment devoid of calcium. This increase varies between 52 and 292% according to Zundel, and is always of an order of magnitude sufficient to rule out statistical errors.

# Thread: Interesting words from Mr. Eisenstein
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-words-from-mr-eisenstein.12056/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Martin — 2014-11-03T16:48:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-words-from-mr-eisenstein.12056/post-146034>

Interesting article on Pollack's book...
[http://charleseisenstein.net/the-waters-of-heterodoxy-g-pollacks-the-fourth-phase-of-water/](http://charleseisenstein.net/the-waters-of-heterodoxy-g-pollacks-the-fourth-phase-of-water/)

From his website: [http://charleseisenstein.net/about-charles/](http://charleseisenstein.net/about-charles/)
"In addition to writing books and essays, I have been doing a tremendous amount of public speaking. Sometimes, especially when I am tired from traveling, I wonder if the world needs yet another man performing “speaker up in front of an audience.” Why not stay at home and use technology? But I find that something happens in person that is irreplaceable. For one thing, at live events I can see the expressions on people’s faces, and I respond to that in real-time, engaging in a sort of dialog. Secondly, my speaking is not just about conveying ideas. Something else rides the vehicle of the words, even if the topic is something mundane like steady-state economics. I sometimes describe my experience as a speaker as being plugged into a field that is not my creation, but is generated by the audience and by something beyond the audience. Besides, I find that my thinking stagnates and my heart atrophies when I am in front of my computer too much. I need to interact with real people, face to face. That’s why I travel and speak."

### Butters — 2014-11-03T18:25:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-time-in-short-light-cycles.12057/post-146037>

I have a question:

At my destination sun sets at 4.30pm now and rises at 7.30. So I have relatively long night hours.
Jack is talking about 7,5-8,5h of sleep is best and too long isn't good. Does it change with the winter season/is it ok to sleep more in winter season?

### kovita — 2014-11-03T19:28:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-time-in-short-light-cycles.12057/post-146042>

i do sleep more in winter and it comes naturally. As the days shorten I find myslef falling asleep as soona s 9-10pm and waking up around 6am. It means I still wake up to the night, so usually i do some yoga or stretching or just meditate while waiting for the sunrise. Do what your body wants as long as it is in harmony with the season (which from my experience comes naturally as soon as you embrace nature and start to listen to the doctor in your head) ;-)

### nicld — 2014-11-03T19:46:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-time-in-short-light-cycles.12057/post-146045>

I sleep longer in the winter too. As soon as I get home now the blue blockers go on and by 8-9 I need to crawl into bed for sleep. Weekends in the winter I sleep a bit later too and wait for the sun to rise to wake me (not a luxury during the week).

### Danco3636 — 2014-11-03T22:39:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-time-in-short-light-cycles.12057/post-146052>
